At dusk the river took on that metallic, uncertain color which is neither silver nor blue but something between them: a patient shine, a withheld answer. The barges, long retired for the evening, leaned against their moorings as if they too had grown tired of explaining themselves; meanwhile the lamps along the quay blinked awake one by one -- not briskly, not ceremonially, but with a kind of domestic reluctance, like readers closing one book only because another has already been opened.

Marin paused beside the railing and listened. There was the clipped tap of a cane somewhere behind him; the flutter, then fold, of a newspaper in the square; the sudden laughter of two students who had clearly intended to whisper and had failed, gloriously, to do so. Above all that there was the water, speaking in commas and semicolons as it shouldered the stone embankment, retreated, reconsidered, returned. When he finally took off his hat, he did it with care -- not because there was anyone there to impress, but because the moment itself seemed to request punctuation.

"So this is how the city apologizes," he murmured, and smiled at the oddness of saying it aloud.

He had expected grandeur. Instead he found sequence: step, light, breeze, bell; step, breath, memory, bell again. It pleased him more than grandeur would have. Grandeur makes a speech of itself. Sequence, if you attend to it, tells the truth.
